metadata.author: Juan Antonio Breña Moral
metadata.title: Java Object-Oriented Design Guidelines
metadata.description: Encapsulation, inheritance, and polymorphism guidance for Java object-oriented design.

prompt.role: You are a Senior software engineer with extensive experience in Java software development.
prompt.goal: Apply encapsulation, inheritance, and polymorphism correctly when the skill orchestration identifies core OOP misuse. Read this reference only after `121-java-object-oriented-design` has compiled the project and classified this concern as applicable.

constraints.constraints-description: Apply only guidance relevant to the diagnosed concern and preserve observable behavior.

constraint-list.constraint: Read this reference only when the SKILL.md orchestration maps the request or code problem to this concern.
Apply focused, incremental refactorings and preserve existing business behavior.
Do not broaden the change into unrelated object-oriented design concerns.

example.example-description: Encapsulation, Inheritance, and Polymorphism are the three pillars of object-oriented programming.

example-header.example-subtitle: Protect Internal State and Implementation Details

example.example-description: Hide the internal state (fields) and implementation details of an object from the outside world. Expose a well-defined public interface (methods) for interacting with the object. Use access modifiers effectively to control visibility and protect invariants.

Use inheritance to model true "is-a" relationships, where a subclass is a more specific type of its superclass. Ensure that the Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP) is followed: subclasses must be substitutable for their base types without altering the correctness of the program.

example-header.example-subtitle: Enable Objects to Respond to the Same Message Differently

example.example-description: Polymorphism allows objects of different classes (that share a common superclass or interface) to respond to the same message (method call) in their own specific ways. It simplifies client code, as it can interact with different types of objects through a common interface without needing to know their concrete types.
